content.full_text:
Fatn Polrucal PnacrrcEs CoivrrvrrssloN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
October 21,20[,4
Brent Meyer
Deparhnent of Transportation
Office of Design West, MS l9
2800 Gateway Oaks Drive, Second Floor
Sacramento, CA 95833
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-04-202
Dear Mr. Meyer:
This Ietter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'.Act').I Your question does not
provide a specific sifuation that could create a conflict ofinterest. Therefore, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance only, as described in more detail
below.2
QUESTION
Would there be a conflict of interest if you take a consulting position with a
private consulting engineering company while working as a se,nior transportation
engineer for Caltrans?
CONCLUSION
There could be a conflict of interest under the Act if you influence your agency's
decisions through your position as a consultant, by making contact with the agency
pursuant to regulation 18702.3(a). This includes your signature on any documents that
go to Caltrans through the consulting firm. However, nothing in the Act or conflict of
interest rules prevent a public official from holding a second position, whether it is in
public or private anployment.
' Govem-"nt Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not proide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed).


File No. I-04-202
Page No. 2
FACTS
Currently, you are a senior transportation engineer working for the California
Department of Transportation (Caltrans), and are considering working for a consulting
engineering company.
As a senior transportation engineer, you work as a roadway designer in
Sacramento. You currently supervise a staffof seven employees and me working on
various projects throughout northem California. You have held your current position for
3.5 years, but have never been a contract manager for a oonsultant contracting firm.
You are considering working for a consulting engineering company, and would be
working for them approximately 2 days a month doing quahty control for a variety of
bridge and roadway projects- Some of this company's clients include other district offices
within Caltrans and local government agencies (cities and counties) as well as private
developers. You would be generating personal income of about $10,000 per year from
the consulting position.
ANALYSIS
Nothing in the Act or the conflict-of-interest rules prevent a public official from
holding a second position, whether it is in public or private employment. In principlg
then, there is no barrier under the Act to the non-governmental ernployment you describe.
There may be occasions when your activities are limited by oonflict-of-interest rules, and
you should consult your ag€ncy's staternent of incompatible activities for guidelines
when considering employment concurrent with yourposition at Caltrans.
The purpose of the Act is to ensure that a public official perforrrs his orher duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by his orher own financial interest or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supportd thepublic official. (Section Sl00l(b).)
The conflict-of-interest provisions are aimed at accomplishing this purpose by preventing
a public official from making participating in making or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which thepublic official has a
financial interest.3 (Section 87i 00.)
A public official has a disqualifoing financial interest in a governmental decision,
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 1870(a).) The Commission has adopted
3 It is impo.tant to note that the Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply on a case-by-case basis and
depend on the specific facts ofeach case. Therefore, we can provide general guidance in this letter, but
whether you have a conflict-of-interest in a given decision will have to be determined when the situation
arises.


File No. I-04-202
Page No. 3
a standard, eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifuing
conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision.4 (Regulation ls700@)(l)-(g).i
Step L: Are you a Public Official?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials, which are
defined as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency." (Section 82048-) As an employee of Caltrans you are a public official.
Step 2: Are you making participating in making or influencing. governmental
decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official is
"making participating in making or using or atterrpting to use his/her official position to
influence a governmental decision" in which he/she knows or has r€ason to know he/she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100; regulation 1S700(bX2).)
Making a govenrment decision entails such things as voting on a matter,
appointing a person, obligating or committing an agencyto a course of action, or
determining not to act because of a conflict of interest. (Regulation 187021.)
Participating in a governmental decision is a broad category and includes advising or
making recoilrm€mdations to the decision-maker or exercising judgment for the purpose
of influencing a decision. (Regul ation 1t702.2.)
The question you raise concerns "influerncin€' agovenrmental decision. There
are two rules as to whether a public official is using or attempting to use his on her
official position to influence a govemmental decision. The first rule applies when the
relevant govemmental decision is within orbefore thepublic official'so*, ug*"y or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency.
(Regulation I 87 02.3 (a).)
"-..the official is atternpting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if...the official contacts, or app&rs before, or otherwise attempts to
influence, any mernber officer, employeg or consultant of the agency. Atternpts
to influence include, but me not limited to, appemances or oontacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.- (Regulation 18702.3(a).)
Under this rule, attempts to influemce a govemmental decision consist of a public
official's appearances or contacts before his or her olvn agency, including any member,
officer, employee or consultant of that agency,on behalf of a business entity, client or
customer. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) Th*, the Act would prohibit you from influencing
other public officials within Caltrans by meeting with them, or making various forms of
a The seventh and eighth steps, which pertain to fire "public generally''exception and the ..legally
required participation" rule, respectively, are not relevant to this general request for information and are not
mentioned further. However, if a decision comes before you where these exceptions may apply, please
contact us for further analysis.


File No. I-04-202
Page No. 4
contact with the public officials within all divisions of Caltrans. Thereforg you might be
disqualified from communicatingwith your agency with regard to the decisions that
come before it through the private consulting firm with which you would be working.
(Farrell Advice Letter, No. I-03-121, RumansoffAdvice Letter, No. I-94-045.)
Ganerally, a public official must not contact other staffwithin his or her official
agency for the purpose of influencing a govemmental decision in which he or she has a
financial interest. @egulation 187a3(a).) Again, "contact" is a broad term, which
includes signing offon a project where your signature appears on a document which will
go before Caltrans, making a phone call to Caltrans employees or simply u.riting a letter,
and thereby influencing a govefirmental decision. However, because we do not have
information on your specific responsibilities as a consultant you would have to
determine, on a case-by-case basis, if you would be'tnfluencing" yourown agency.s
The second rulepotentially applies when the governmental decision is within or
before an agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency- (Regulation
18702.3(b).) Under this rule, "the official is attempting to use his or her official position
to influence the decision i{ for the purpose of influeincing the decision, the official acts or
purports to act on behalf o{, or as the representative o{, his or her agency to any member,
officer, employee or consultant of an agency." (IbA Thereforg you would be prohibited
from going before another agency while representing yourself as a public official of
Caltrans for purposes related to your consulting position. (Larmore Advice Letter, No.
A-0u27s).
Step 3: What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts ofinterest may
arise are defined within section 87103 of the Act and regulations l871i - 18703.5.
A public official has an economic interest in abusiness e,ntity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect invesknent of two thousand dollars or more (section
87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a).); or in which he or she is a director, officer,
parbrer, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section
87 I 03(b); regulation I 8703. I (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of two thousand dollars or more (section s7103@);
regulation 18703.2);
a
o
5 We also note section STlA(a)provides, "[n]o public official of a state agency shall, for
compensation, act as an agent or attomey for, or otherwise represent, any other person by making any
formal or informal appearance before, or any oral or written communication to, his or her state agency or
any officer or employee thereof, if the appearance or communication is for the purpose of influencing a
decision on a contract, grilt, loan, license, permit, or other entitlement for use.i' You have not provided
any facts regarding the possible application of this statute. However, you should be cognizant of this
prohibition.


o
FileNo. I-04-202
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to five hundred dollms ($500) ormore *ittritt
12 months prior to the time in which the relevant govennmental decision was
made. (Section 87 I 03(c); regulation I 3703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any souroe of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to three hundred forty dollars or more within 12 months prior
to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation lB7O3-4.)
You have an economic interest in the consulting firm arising from your salary as a
consultant provided you have received from the consulting firrr five hundred dollars or
more in the twelve months prior to when the relevant decision is made. (Section
87103(c).) In addition, you have an ooonomic interest in the consulting firm by virtue of
your employment relationship.
Step 4: Are your economic interests directly or indirectty involved in the
governmental decision ?
A person's economic interests are directly involved in a decision when the person
is "...the subject of, the proceeding concerring the decision before the official- that
"involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial orrevocation of anylicense, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject p€trson.,, (Regulation rcla.{.1 ff a
person who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly involved in a
goverrmental decision, theperson is deemed to be indirectlyinvolved. (Rqgulation
18704.1(b).)
If the consulting firm you would be employed by is the subject of a proceeding
involving "the issuance, renewal, approval, dexrial or revocation of any lice,nse, permit, or
other entitlement to, or conhact with" the agency for which you are a public official, and
you have also represented yourself as an employee of the consulting firm to your agency,
there could be a direct involvement betweem the source of income and the governmental
decision, if there is a material financial effect on the consulting firm.
However, if such a situation were to arise, you have indicated that you would for
ask for the work to be reassigned. You would need to make this decision on a case-by-
case basis and could request an advice letter with specific facts relevant to that sifuation.
If the private consulting firm is not considered directly involved in the
goveflrmental decision under the rules stated abovg they are indirectlyinvolved.
Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard?
..Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official,s
agency, is deemed material." (Regulation 13705.3.) The standard for determining


File No. l-04-202
Page No. 6
materiality of a direct economic interest is the "onepenny''rule. If the income of the
business entity, in this case the consulting firm, were to increase or decrease by one
pemy, the economic interest is deemed material.
The materiality standard applicable to a business entity that is indirectly involved
in a governmental decision is set forth in regulation 18705-3(c), which lists thresholds for
business entities that fall within a gross revenue category or meet the rquirements to be
listed on the New York Stock Exchangg NASDAQ orAmerican Stock Exchange. If
these requirem€nts do not apply a third category states:
*(4) ...the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 ormore; or,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.- (Regulation
l870s.l(c)(4XA>(c).)
Since, you do not provide any facts relevant to the size ofthe consulting firm you
would be working for or any decision you could be involved in, you would have to
determine if there is a material financial effect to the business on a case-by-case basis.
'Vy'e have enclosed a copy ofregulation 18705.1 in order for you to determine ifthere
could be an indirect material financial effect upon the conzulting firm based upon the size
of the business entity.
Step 6: Is it substantially likely that the governmental decision will meet the
materiality standards?
A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is "substantially likely''
that one or more of the materiality standards will be met. @egulation 13706.) If it is
reasonably foreseeable that the consulting firrr would be materially affected, basod upon
the above standards you would be required to disqualifo yourself from any involvernent
between Caltrans and the consulting firm.
However, you have indicated that you would ask for the work to be reassigned if
there could be a potential conflict of interest, and at that time, you could also request an
advice letter with specific facts to determine whether there is a conflict of interest.


File No. I-04-202
Page No. 7
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s66A.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Cormsel
By
KaruMazzlttcc,a
d
Intern, Legal
Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would there be a conflict of interest if you take a consulting position with a
private consulting engineering company while working as a se,nior transportation
engineer for Caltrans?

CONCLUSION: There could be a conflict of interest under the Act if you influence your agency's
decisions through your position as a consultant, by making contact with the agency
pursuant to regulation 18702.3(a). This includes your signature on any documents that
go to Caltrans through the consulting firm. However, nothing in the Act or conflict of
interest rules prevent a public official from holding a second position, whether it is in
public or private anployment.
' Govem-"nt Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations.

formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
embedding.first_500_words: Fatn Polrucal PnacrrcEs CoivrrvrrssloN 428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 October 21,20[,4 Brent Meyer Deparhnent of Transportation Office of Design West, MS l9 2800 Gateway Oaks Drive, Second Floor Sacramento, CA 95833 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our FiIe No. I-04-202 Dear Mr. Meyer: This Ietter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'.Act').I Your question does not provide a specific sifuation that could create a conflict ofinterest. Therefore, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance only, as described in more detail below.2 QUESTION Would there be a conflict of interest if you take a consulting position with a private consulting engineering company while working as a se,nior transportation engineer for Caltrans? CONCLUSION There could be a conflict of interest under the Act if you influence your agency's decisions through your position as a consultant, by making contact with the agency pursuant to regulation 18702.3(a). This includes your signature on any documents that go to Caltrans through the consulting firm. However, nothing in the Act or conflict of interest rules prevent a public official from holding a second position, whether it is in public or private anployment. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not proide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed). File No. I-04-202 Page No. 2 FACTS Currently, you are a senior transportation engineer working for the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans), and are considering working for a consulting engineering company. As a senior transportation engineer, you work as a roadway designer in Sacramento. You currently supervise a staffof seven employees and me working on various projects throughout northem California. You have held your current position for 3.5 years, but have never been a contract manager for a oonsultant contracting firm. You are considering working for a consulting engineering company, and would be working for them approximately 2 days a month doing quahty control for a variety of bridge and roadway projects- Some of this company's clients include other district offices within Caltrans and local government agencies (cities and counties) as well as private developers. You would be generating personal income of about $10,000 per year from the consulting position. ANALYSIS Nothing in the Act or the conflict-of-interest rules prevent a public official from holding a second position, whether it is in public or private employment. In principlg then, there is no barrier under the Act to the non-governmental ernployment you describe. There may be occasions when your activities are limited by oonflict-of-interest rules, and you should consult your ag€ncy's staternent of incompatible activities for guidelines when considering employment concurrent with yourposition at Caltrans.
